items.q: What follows 'si' (if) in a conditional sentence like 'If I were rich, I would travel'?
items.correct: Imperfect tense (Si j'ÉTAIS riche, je voyagerais).
items.why: A classic error - students put conditional after si. The rule: si + IMPERFECT in the if-clause, conditional in the result clause. Never 'si je serais' - always 'si j'étais'.

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.

items.q: Which verbs take 'être' as their auxiliary in the passé composé?
items.correct: Movement/state-change verbs (DR & MRS VAN DER TRAMP) and all reflexive verbs.
items.why: Students forget reflexives. Both groups use être: aller/venir/monter, etc. AND every reflexive verb (je me suis lavé). With être, the past participle agrees with the subject.

items.q: Which tense follows 'si' in a hypothetical sentence with conditional present?
items.why: It's Imperfect. “Future” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Where is the past historic mainly used today?
items.why: It's Written literature. “Spoken conversation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many endings make up the past historic paradigm for -er verbs?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
